evidence: Runtime seam design is repeated as large callback bags across modules (`docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` Runtime/StartupRuntime/StartramRuntime, `startup_orchestrator.go` startupRuntime/startBackgroundServicesRuntime, `shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` workflowRuntime).

Many public APIs are duplicated as `X` + `XWithRuntime(default...)` wrappers, increasing call-path count with little behavior added (`broadcast/collectors/collectors.go`, `importer/importer.go`, `shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `main.go`).

A formal config-read abstraction exists (`config/config_view.go`), but key flows still read mutable global config directly (`rectify/rectify.go`, `routines/healthcheck/disk.go`, `docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`), indicating leaky boundaries.

Transition/event lifecycle handling is hand-coded across multiple workflow modules (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/startram.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`) with repeated loading/success/error/clear patterns instead of a shared state-machine abstraction.

Log-file selection logic is duplicated between support and logstream modules (`type LogFile`, `-part-` filename parsing, date/part sorting) in `goseg/handler/system/support.go` and `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go`.

Wi-Fi and access-point orchestration spans shell command execution, service lifecycle, polling loops, and HTTP/captive behavior in overlapping modules (`goseg/system/wifi.go`, `goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint.go`), indicating weak boundary separation.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtimes create high indirection cost and duplicated seam plumbing.
evidence: `runtime_seams.go` defines multiple wide structs of function fields plus many `With*` option-mergers that manually copy non-nil callbacks.
`startup_orchestrator.go` has broad runtime structs and a long `withDefaults`/`validate` sequence that repeats dependency wiring logic.
`operations_runtime.go` repeats this seam style (`workflowRuntime`) with nil-guard errors for missing callbacks, shifting correctness checks to runtime instead of constructor-time.
findings.suggestion: Replace callback bags with narrower capability interfaces per workflow boundary (e.g., lifecycle, transition publish, config read/write), and construct them with explicit required dependencies so missing wiring fails at compile-time or constructor-time. Keep optional hooks in small, local option structs instead of package-wide mega-runtimes.

findings.summary: Dual API pattern (`X` + `XWithRuntime`) adds wrapper depth without consistent leverage.
evidence: `collectors.go` repeatedly exposes thin wrappers that call `...WithRuntime(defaultCollectorRuntime(), ...)`.
`importer.go` and `operations_runtime.go` follow the same pattern for many entrypoints, creating two near-identical call paths per behavior.
`main.go` has parallel default-runtime wrappers (`bootstrapSubsystems`/`bootstrapSubsystemsWithRuntime`, `startServer`/`startServerWithRuntime`) with minimal policy differences.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one public API shape: keep production entrypoints simple and move test seams to unexported constructors or package-level dependency injection points. Reduce exported wrapper pairs to only places where runtime substitution is meaningful at module boundaries.

findings.summary: Config snapshot abstraction is not consistently used, so callers bypass intended boundary.
evidence: `config_view.go` introduces `ConfigView`/snapshot methods intended to avoid direct mutable global reads.
`rectify.go` and `disk.go` still call `config.Conf()` directly in transition/loop logic rather than consuming a snapshot view dependency.
`runtime_seams.go` continues exposing direct config functions (`ConfFn`, `RuntimeContextSnapshot` closures), so consumers can bypass the view contract.
findings.suggestion: Enforce a single read abstraction for orchestration and long-running loops: pass `ConfigView` snapshots (or narrow settings snapshots) into workflows and remove direct `config.Conf()` access from non-config packages. Keep writes behind explicit mutation ports.

findings.summary: Routines facade duplicates logstream API as pass-through wrappers with no abstraction value.
evidence: `routines/logs.go` functions are mostly one-line forwarding wrappers to `logstream` plus repeated nil-context normalization.
`startup_orchestrator.go` uses these wrapper names as subsystem hooks, but the wrappers do not add policy, translation, or lifecycle guarantees beyond delegation.
Both packages now carry parallel API surfaces (`X` and `XWithContext`) increasing maintenance surface for the same behavior.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the facade and depend directly on `logstream` at call sites, or give the facade a real contract (policy, metrics, supervision behavior) and collapse duplicate function variants behind a single context-first API.

findings.summary: Transition lifecycle behavior is duplicated across ship and startram workflows instead of centralized in one orchestration model.
evidence: `urbit_operations.go` repeatedly emits transition states inline (for example `pack`, `packMeld`, `deleteShip`, `togglePower`, `rollChop`) with per-function start/error/success/clear handling.
`startram.go` separately re-implements transition sequencing via direct `startram.PublishEvent(...)` calls and `PublishTransitionWithPolicy(...)` wrappers for restart/toggle/register/endpoint flows.
`rectify.go` contains another distinct transition-application path (`applyTransitionUpdate`, `set*Transition` functions) that updates broadcast state independently from workflow emitters.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared transition orchestration component (typed state progression + terminal policy + clear timing) used by both shipworkflow and rectify paths; migrate existing operations to declarative transition plans instead of inline event strings.

findings.summary: Support and logstream modules duplicate the same log filename parsing/sorting structure.
evidence: Both files define a `LogFile` struct and parse `*.log` names by splitting on `-part-` (`support.go` and `logs.go`).
Both implementations sort by date and part number with nearly identical `sort.Slice` comparators.
This logic diverges only in retention count/consumer behavior, not parsing strategy, which indicates reusable shared logic was not extracted.
findings.suggestion: Extract log filename parsing and recency ordering into a shared package (for example `logger/logfiles`) and have support/reporting and cleanup routines call the same API with different retention policies.

findings.summary: Wi-Fi/C2C flow mixes infrastructure control, policy sequencing, and API surface across overlapping modules.
evidence: `wifi.go` combines loop/state management (`wifiInfoLoop`), system service control (`systemctl` enable/start/stop), network connect/reboot flows (`C2CConnect`), and API endpoints (`CaptiveAPI`, `CaptivePortal`) in one module.
`accesspoint.go` combines runtime normalization, dependency checks, process probing/matcher internals, and shell execution in the same package.
Cross-calls (`wifi.go` driving AP lifecycle while also owning C2C policy) indicate orchestration and adapter boundaries are not cleanly separated.
findings.suggestion: Split into explicit layers: Wi-Fi policy service (C2C state machine), AP adapter (hostapd/dnsmasq lifecycle), and HTTP/captive adapter; keep shell/process concerns behind interfaces and move sequencing rules into one coordinator.
